64 Mercer Street [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey") , [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") , U.S.

(**PTSem**), officially **The Theological Seminary of the Presbyterian Church**,[[8]](#cite_note-8) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [school of theology](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") in [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). Established in 1812, it is the second-oldest seminary in the United States, founded under the auspices of [Archibald Alexander](/wiki/Archibald_Alexander "Archibald Alexander"), the General Assembly of the [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"), and the College of New Jersey (now [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")).[[9]](#cite_note-ptsem1-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) It is also the largest of [ten seminaries](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.)_seminaries "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.) seminaries") associated with the Presbyterian Church.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Private school of theology in New Jersey, US

Not to be confused with [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University").

Princeton Theological Seminary

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Princenton_Theological_Seminary_Campus_Map_%284514614069%29.jpg/250px-Princenton_Theological_Seminary_Campus_Map_%284514614069%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princenton_Theological_Seminary_Campus_Map_(4514614069).jpg)

**Princeton Theological Seminary** (**PTSem**), officially **The Theological Seminary of the Presbyterian Church**,[[8]](#cite_note-8) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [school of theology](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") in [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). Established in 1812, it is the second-oldest seminary in the United States, founded under the auspices of [Archibald Alexander](/wiki/Archibald_Alexander "Archibald Alexander"), the General Assembly of the [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"), and the College of New Jersey (now [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")).[[9]](#cite_note-ptsem1-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) It is also the largest of [ten seminaries](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.)_seminaries "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.) seminaries") associated with the Presbyterian Church.

The seminary operates the largest theological library in the United States and the second largest in the world (after the Vatican in Rome). It maintains a number of special collections, including the [Karl Barth Research Collection](/wiki/Karl_Barth "Karl Barth") in the [Center for Barth Studies](/wiki/Karl_Barth#Center_for_Barth_Studies "Karl Barth"). The seminary also managed an endowment of $1.459 billion in 2022,[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) making it the third-wealthiest institution of [higher learning](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") in the state of New Jersey after Princeton University and [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the 1980s, it enrolled about 900 students, but as of Fall 2023, the seminary enrolls approximately 276 FTE students.[[2]](#cite_note-ATS-2) While around 26 percent of them are candidates for ministry specifically in the Presbyterian Church, the majority are completing such candidature in other denominations, pursuing careers in academia across a number of different disciplines, or receiving training for other, non-theological fields altogether.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The Seminary holds academic reciprocity with [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") as well as the [Westminster Choir College](/wiki/Westminster_Choir_College "Westminster Choir College") of [Rider University](/wiki/Rider_University "Rider University"), [New Brunswick Theological Seminary](/wiki/New_Brunswick_Theological_Seminary "New Brunswick Theological Seminary"), the [Jewish Theological Seminary](/wiki/Jewish_Theological_Seminary_of_America "Jewish Theological Seminary of America"), and the [Rutgers School of Social Work](/wiki/Rutgers_School_of_Social_Work "Rutgers School of Social Work").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg/330px-Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)

Princeton Seminary in the 1800s

The plan to establish a theological seminary in Princeton was in the interests of advancing and extending the theological curriculum. The educational intention was to go beyond the liberal arts course by setting up a postgraduate, professional school in theology. The plan met with enthusiastic approval on the part of authorities at the College of New Jersey, later to become [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), for they were coming to see that specialized training in theology required more attention than they could give. The General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church established the Theological Seminary at Princeton, New Jersey, in 1812, with the support of the directors of the nearby College of New Jersey, as the second graduate theological school in the United States. The seminary remains an institution of the [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"), being the largest of the ten theological seminaries affiliated with the 1.1-million-member denomination.[[9]](#cite_note-ptsem1-9)[[14]](#cite_note-comparativestats-14)

In 1812, the seminary had three students and [Archibald Alexander](/wiki/Archibald_Alexander "Archibald Alexander") was its first professor. By 1815 the number of students had gradually increased, and work began on a building: Alexander Hall was designed by [John McComb Jr.](/wiki/John_McComb_Jr. "John McComb Jr."), a New York architect, and opened in 1817. The original cupola was added in 1827, but it burned in 1913 and was replaced in 1926. The building was simply called "Seminary" until 1893, when it was officially named Alexander Hall. Since its founding, Princeton Seminary has graduated approximately 14,000 men and women who have served the church in many capacities, from pastoral ministry and pastoral care to missionary work, Christian education and leadership in the academy and business.

The seminary became known during the 19th and early 20th centuries for its defense of [Calvinistic](/wiki/Calvinistic "Calvinistic") [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), a tradition that became known as [Princeton Theology](/wiki/Princeton_Theology "Princeton Theology") and greatly influenced [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") during the period. Some of the institution's figures active in this movement included [Charles Hodge](/wiki/Charles_Hodge "Charles Hodge"), [B.B. Warfield](/wiki/B._B._Warfield "B. B. Warfield"), [J. Gresham Machen](/wiki/J_Gresham_Machen "J Gresham Machen"), and [Geerhardus Vos](/wiki/Geerhardus_Vos "Geerhardus Vos").

### Liberalism and split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Liberalism and split")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Princeton_Seminary_class_of_1922_%289314078919%29.jpg/250px-Princeton_Seminary_class_of_1922_%289314078919%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Seminary_class_of_1922_(9314078919).jpg)

Princeton Seminary class of 1922

In response to the increasing influence of [theological liberalism](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") in the 1920s and the [fundamentalist–modernist controversy](/wiki/Fundamentalist%E2%80%93Modernist_Controversy "Fundamentalist–Modernist Controversy") at the institution, several theologians left to form the [Westminster Theological Seminary](/wiki/Westminster_Theological_Seminary "Westminster Theological Seminary") under the leadership of [J. Gresham Machen](/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen "J. Gresham Machen").

The college was later the center of the [fundamentalist–modernist controversy](/wiki/Fundamentalist%E2%80%93Modernist_Controversy "Fundamentalist–Modernist Controversy") of the 1920s and 1930s.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 1929, the seminary was reorganized along modernist lines, and in response, Machen, along with three of his colleagues: [Oswald T. Allis](/wiki/Oswald_T._Allis "Oswald T. Allis"), [Robert Dick Wilson](/wiki/Robert_Dick_Wilson "Robert Dick Wilson") and [Cornelius Van Til](/wiki/Cornelius_Van_Til "Cornelius Van Til"), resigned, with Machen, Allis and Wilson founding [Westminster Theological Seminary](/wiki/Westminster_Theological_Seminary "Westminster Theological Seminary") in [Glenside, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Glenside,_Pennsylvania "Glenside, Pennsylvania"). In 1958, Princeton became a seminary of the [United Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A](/wiki/United_Presbyterian_Church_in_the_U.S.A. "United Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A.")., following a merger between the Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A. and the [United Presbyterian Church of North America](/wiki/United_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "United Presbyterian Church of North America"), and in 1983, it would become a seminary of the Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.) after the merger between the UPCUSA and the [Presbyterian Church in the U.S.](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_U.S. "Presbyterian Church in the U.S.")

### Ties to slavery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ties to slavery")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Alexander_Hall_and_sign_at_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg/250px-Alexander_Hall_and_sign_at_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexander_Hall_and_sign_at_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)

Alexander Hall and Sign

In 2019, the seminary announced that it would spend $27 million on "scholarships and other initiatives to address its historical ties to slavery".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Stuart_Hall_at_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg/250px-Stuart_Hall_at_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stuart_Hall_at_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)

Stuart Hall. The main classroom building of the Princeton Theological Seminary, designed by [William Appleton Potter](/wiki/William_Appleton_Potter "William Appleton Potter") in [Venetian Gothic](/wiki/Venetian_Gothic_architecture "Venetian Gothic architecture") style. Built in 1876.

Princeton Theological Seminary has been accredited by the [Commission on Accrediting of the Association of Theological Schools (ATS)](/wiki/American_Theological_Library_Association "American Theological Library Association") since 1938 and by the [Middle States Commission on Higher Education](/wiki/Middle_States_Commission_on_Higher_Education "Middle States Commission on Higher Education") since 1968.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[1]](#cite_note-effectiveness-1)

### Degree programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Degree programs")]

* [Master of Divinity](/wiki/Master_of_Divinity "Master of Divinity") (M.Div.)
* [Masters of Arts](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") (MA)
* [Master of Arts in Theological Studies](/wiki/Master_of_Arts_in_Theological_Studies "Master of Arts in Theological Studies") (MATS)
* [Master of Theology](/wiki/Master_of_Theology "Master of Theology") (Th.M.)
* [Doctor of Ministry](/wiki/Doctor_of_Ministry "Doctor of Ministry") (D.Min.), offered from 1975 to 2005, having been replaced with Ph.D. in Practical Theology
* [Doctor of Philosophy](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") (Ph.D.), although the [Doctor of Theology](/wiki/Doctor_of_Theology "Doctor of Theology") was previously awarded
* Dual M.Div./MA in Christian Education with foci in Youth & Young Adults, Teaching Ministry, or Spiritual Development
* Dual M.Div./MSW in partnership with Rutgers School of Social Work[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Wright_Library%2C_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg/250px-Wright_Library%2C_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wright_Library,_Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)

Wright Library

The Wright Library is a destination for visiting scholars from around the world. The current library building was completed in 2013 and was renamed on October 13, 2021, after [Theodore S. Wright](/wiki/Theodore_S._Wright "Theodore S. Wright"), the first African American to graduate from Princeton Theological Seminary.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The library has over 1,252,503 bound volumes, pamphlets, and microfilms.[[20]](#cite_note-20) It receives about 2,100 journals, annual reports of church bodies and learned societies, bulletins, transactions, and periodically issued indices, abstracts, and bibliographies. The libraries are:

* Princeton Theological Seminary Library ("The Wright Library") was opened in 2013 and holds the bulk of the seminary's collection. The library is also home to the Center for Barth Studies,[[21]](#cite_note-21) the Reigner Reading Room,[[22]](#cite_note-22) and special collections including the [Abraham Kuyper](/wiki/Abraham_Kuyper "Abraham Kuyper") collection of Dutch Reformed Protestantism and personal libraries of theologians like [Ashbel Green](/wiki/Ashbel_Green "Ashbel Green"), [William Buell Sprague](/wiki/William_Buell_Sprague "William Buell Sprague"), [Joseph Addison Alexander](/wiki/Joseph_Addison_Alexander "Joseph Addison Alexander"), [Alexander Balloch Grosart](/wiki/Alexander_Balloch_Grosart "Alexander Balloch Grosart"), [William Henry Green](/wiki/William_Henry_Green "William Henry Green"), [Samuel Miller](/wiki/Samuel_Miller_(theologian) "Samuel Miller (theologian)"), and [B. B. Warfield](/wiki/B._B._Warfield "B. B. Warfield").[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* Speer Library was opened in 1957 and named in honor of the renowned missionary statesman [Robert E. Speer](/wiki/Robert_E._Speer "Robert E. Speer"). It was closed in late 2010 and was replaced by the new library.
* Henry Luce III Library, dedicated in 1994 and named in honor of a distinguished trustee, [Henry W. Luce](/wiki/Henry_W._Luce "Henry W. Luce"), has 350,000 volumes and 250 readers. This library merged with Wright Library in 2013.

### Rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rankings")]

Given its status as an autonomous [postgraduate institution](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education"), Princeton Seminary does not appear in most global or national rankings for universities and colleges. As a [graduate school](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school"), however, it does see such ranking on occasion. In 2020, it was ranked #53 nationwide – tied with [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") and [University of Florida](/wiki/University_of_Florida "University of Florida") – for the field of history by the *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[24]](#cite_note-24) It was also rated at A+ by the American rankings and review company [Niche](/wiki/Niche_(company) "Niche (company)") in 2020.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The journal *[First Things](/wiki/First_Things "First Things")*, an organ of the Institute on Religion and Public Life in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), ranked Princeton Seminary fifth among American graduate programs in theology, in 2012.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Seminary Chapel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Seminary Chapel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Miller_Chapel_%28front%29.jpg/250px-Miller_Chapel_%28front%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miller_Chapel_(front).jpg)

Miller Chapel

Main article: [Miller Chapel](/wiki/Miller_Chapel "Miller Chapel")

Built in 1834, Princeton Seminary's chapel was named to honor [Samuel Miller](/wiki/Samuel_Miller_(theologian) "Samuel Miller (theologian)"), the second professor at the seminary. It was designed in the [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture") style by [Charles Steadman](/wiki/Charles_Steadman "Charles Steadman"), who also designed the nearby [Nassau Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Nassau_Presbyterian_Church "Nassau Presbyterian Church"). Originally located beside Alexander Hall, it was moved in 1933 toward the center of the campus, its steps now leading down onto the seminary's main quad. Miller Chapel underwent a complete renovation in 2000, with the addition of the Joe R. Engle Organ.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

On January 18, 2022, members of the Association of Black Seminarians physically removed the sign naming the chapel "Miller Chapel" and held a protest calling for the trustees to rename the chapel because of Samuel Miller's direct ties to slavery.[[29]](#cite_note-29) On January 25, 2022, the Board of Trustees of Princeton Seminary voted to rename Miller Chapel in light of the protest. "This decision followed thoughtful deliberation by the Board of Trustees, and it is part of their commitment to the ongoing work of confession and repentance that was part of the historical audit on slavery."[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Navigating the Waters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Navigating the Waters")]

In 2011, Princeton Theological Seminary's Office of Multicultural Relations and The Kaleidoscope Institute worked together to initiate an effort known as "Navigating the Waters", a program designed to promote cultural proficiency and diversity competency in faculty, staff, and students.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Research

### Center for Barth Studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Center for Barth Studies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/GENERAL_VIEW_OF_FRONT_FACADE_-_Princeton_Theological_Seminary%2C_Mercer_Street%2C_Princeton%2C_Mercer_County%2C_NJ_HABS_NJ%2C11-PRINT%2C18A-2.tif/lossy-page1-250px-GENERAL_VIEW_OF_FRONT_FACADE_-_Princeton_Theological_Seminary%2C_Mercer_Street%2C_Princeton%2C_Mercer_County%2C_NJ_HABS_NJ%2C11-PRINT%2C18A-2.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:GENERAL_VIEW_OF_FRONT_FACADE_-_Princeton_Theological_Seminary,_Mercer_Street,_Princeton,_Mercer_County,_NJ_HABS_NJ,11-PRINT,18A-2.tif)

Alexander Hall. The original building of the Princeton Theological Seminary, patterned after Nassau Hall, and designed by [John McComb Jr.](/wiki/John_McComb_Jr. "John McComb Jr.") Built in 1814.

The Center for Barth Studies was established at Princeton Seminary in 1997 and is administered by a board of seminary faculty. The Center sponsors conferences, research opportunities, discussion groups, and publications that seek to advance understanding of the theology of [Karl Barth](/wiki/Karl_Barth "Karl Barth") (1886–1968), the [German Swiss](/wiki/German-speaking_Switzerland "German-speaking Switzerland") professor and pastor widely regarded as the greatest theologian of the 20th century. The [Karl Barth Research Collection](/w/index.php?title=Karl_Barth_Research_Collection&action=edit&redlink=1 "Karl Barth Research Collection (page does not exist)"), part of Special Collections in the Princeton Theological Seminary Libraries, supports the scholarly activities of the [Center for Barth Studies](/w/index.php?title=Center_for_Barth_Studies&action=edit&redlink=1 "Center for Barth Studies (page does not exist)"). The Karl Barth Research Collection is acquiring a collection of writings by and about Karl Barth. Although many volumes are still needed, the Research Collection has already acquired Barth's most important works in German and English, several first editions, and an original hand-written manuscript by Karl Barth.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Abraham Kuyper Center for Public Theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Abraham Kuyper Center for Public Theology")]

The key aspect of the Abraham Kuyper Center for Public Theology is the Abraham Kuyper Collection of Dutch Reformed Protestantism in the library's Special Collections, which focuses on the theology and history of [Dutch Reformed Protestantism](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_Reformed_Protestantism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dutch Reformed Protestantism (page does not exist)") since the nineteenth century and features a sizable assemblage of primary and secondary sources by and about [Abraham Kuyper](/wiki/Abraham_Kuyper "Abraham Kuyper"). The center maintains in partnership with the [Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam](/wiki/Vrije_Universiteit_Amsterdam "Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam") an online [database of secondary literature about Abraham Kuyper](http://www.kuyperresearch.org/).

The center has also established an annual event organized to award the Abraham Kuyper Prize for Excellence in Reformed Theology and Public Life, during which the recipient delivers an address. The Abraham Kuyper Consultation, a series of further lectures, takes place on the following day.

In 2017, there was a controversy surrounding the plan to award the Kuyper Prize to [Tim Keller](/wiki/Tim_Keller_(pastor) "Tim Keller (pastor)"), then Pastor of [Redeemer Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Redeemer_Presbyterian_Church_(New_York_City) "Redeemer Presbyterian Church (New York City)") in New York City. A group of students and faculty protested that Keller should not receive the award due to his non-affirming views regarding [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBT_clergy_in_Christianity "LGBT clergy in Christianity") and women clergy.[[33]](#cite_note-33) President Barnes initially defended awarding Keller the prize before changing his position.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Keller withdrew himself from consideration for the prize and still delivered his lecture. While drawing support from some quarters, the decision to not award Keller the prize also drew criticism in the [*Wall Street Journal*](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")[[35]](#cite_note-35) and [*Washington Post*](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Center of Theological Inquiry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Center of Theological Inquiry")]

In 1978, Princeton Theological Seminary's Board of Trustees established the [Center of Theological Inquiry](/wiki/Center_of_Theological_Inquiry "Center of Theological Inquiry") (CTI) as an independent, [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenical "Ecumenical") institution for advanced theological research, "to inquire into the relationship between theological disciplines, [and of these with] ... both human and natural sciences, to inquire into the relationship between diverse religious traditions ..., to inquire into the present state of religious consciousness in the modern world, and to examine such other facets of religion in the modern world as may be appropriate ..."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Today, the CTI has its own board, funding, mission and staff. The present director is [Tom Greggs](/wiki/Tom_Greggs "Tom Greggs") and the associate director is Joshua Mauldin.

### Journals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Journals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Princeton_Theological_Review%2C_Volume_1%2C_Number_1_%281903%29.djvu/page1-250px-Princeton_Theological_Review%2C_Volume_1%2C_Number_1_%281903%29.djvu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Theological_Review,_Volume_1,_Number_1_(1903).djvu)

Princeton Theological Review, Volume 1, Number 1 (1903)

*Theology Today* is a peer-reviewed, quarterly journal of Christian theology founded in 1944.

*Koinonia Journal* is published annually by doctoral students at Princeton Theological Seminary. The publication and its annual forum promote written and face-to-face interdisciplinary discussion about issues in theology and the study of religion. It is distributed to well over 100 libraries worldwide.

*Princeton Theological Review* is a student-run, annual and online journal that exists to serve students within the Princeton Theological Seminary body as well as the wider theological community. It is distributed to well over 100 libraries worldwide.

### Frederick Buechner Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Princeton_Theological_Seminary&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Frederick Buechner Prize")]

Acclaimed writer and theologian [Frederick Buechner](/wiki/Frederick_Buechner "Frederick Buechner") has long standing ties to Princeton Theological Seminary and the seminary has honored him with the creation of the Buechner Prize for Writing. Princeton sponsored and hosted the Buechner Writing Workshop in June 2015. Also, Princeton Theological Seminary has given copies of Buechner's *Telling the Truth* to students as part of their graduation.
